evidence: GrammarConfigLoader Javadoc comments are purposeful and domain-specific: the class Javadoc explains the JSON structure with an example, parseRelation explains position derivation, validateNoLegacyKeys explains the copulas prohibition — none of these restate the code

RelationConfig.posGroupForPrefix comment: 'tag= fallback supports legacy grammar files that use tag= instead of xpos=. Kept to remain compatible with older grammar configs not yet migrated to xpos=.' — explains WHY, not WHAT
ExploreOptions Javadoc explains the Explore/Exploration naming asymmetry and clarifies when nounsPerSeed applies — addresses a non-obvious decision
LogDiceCalculator is clean: one formula, one overload, one utility method, no defensive sprawl around the core computation
RelationConfig.substituteHeadword is a 1-line private method that does nothing but call CqlUtils.substituteAtPosition with identical parameters — a pass-through wrapper with no added policy
ExploreOptions.includeExamples is never used in any production code path; always constructed with false and never read — a dead field that inflates the API surface
RelationEdgeType.label() returns a stable JSON string; RelationEdgeType.toString() returns the same string; the toString override is redundant given that callers universally use label()

BlackLabQueryExecutor method-level null/empty guards appear on executeDependencyPattern and executeDependencyPatternWithPos but not on executeSurfacePattern and executeCqlQuery — the defensive-check pattern is inconsistently applied, suggesting copy-paste from one method to another without systematic design

SemanticFieldExplorer Javadoc class comment is extensive (35+ lines) explaining the two exploration modes with a worked example for 'house' — this is genuine explanatory documentation for a non-trivial algorithm, not restating boilerplate

Main.java contains 'System.out.println("==========================================" )' style banner output — a cosmetic artifact that is functional (CLI user feedback) but marginally characteristic of AI-assisted output

GrammarConfigLoader.missingConfigException: 'private static java.io.FileNotFoundException missingConfigException(Path p) { return new java.io.FileNotFoundException(...); }' — a factory method whose sole purpose is constructing one exception type, called from one site with a comment '// always call with throw'; the comment indicates the method's design is awkward rather than intentional
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: substituteHeadword pass-through, includeExamples dead field, redundant RelationEdgeType.toString, and inconsistent null-guard application pattern are the main AI-debt markers.

strengths: Algorithmic Javadoc comments (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateQueryHelper phase comments, GrammarConfigLoader structure doc) explain WHY and HOW, not just WHAT — genuine documentation rather than restatement
LogDiceCalculator has no defensive sprawl: null inputs are handled via a documented sentinel (UNCOMPUTABLE = NaN), the formula is direct, and the long overload is a clean delegation
RelationConfig's TODO comments for NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT and ADJECTIVE_PATTERN explicitly identify what needs to change and why — actionable technical debt markers, not filler
CqlUtils methods have example-based Javadoc that demonstrates input/output — this is the right use of documentation for non-trivial parsing utilities
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: A small cluster of AI-hallmark patterns — one dead field, one pass-through wrapper, one redundant toString, and one inconsistent null-check pattern — rather than systemic boilerplate; the deliberate code is clearly distinguishable from the generated-style artifacts.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase shows clear intentionality in its core algorithmic and documentation choices: class Javadocs explain non-obvious decisions, utility methods earn their existence, and TODOs are actionable. The AI-debt markers are confined to a small set of identified artifacts: the includeExamples dead field, the substituteHeadword 1-line wrapper, the redundant RelationEdgeType.toString(), and the copy-paste null-guard pattern in BlackLabQueryExecutor. None of these are serious defects, but their co-occurrence in an otherwise deliberate codebase suggests AI-assisted code generation that was not fully reviewed. The score reflects 'above average, recognizable AI debris cluster without systemic contamination'.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.substituteHeadword is a 1-line pass-through wrapper with no added logic, policy, or meaningful name — a textbook AI-generated wrapper artifact
evidence: 'private static String substituteHeadword(String pattern, String headword, int headPosition) { return CqlUtils.substituteAtPosition(pattern, headword, headPosition); }'
Called from exactly one site (getFullPattern) with identical parameter values
No precondition, no null guard, no logging, no name enrichment — pure delegation
issues.suggestion: Inline the CqlUtils.substituteAtPosition call directly in getFullPattern and delete substituteHeadword.

issues.summary: RelationEdgeType.toString() overrides to return label() — identical to the label() method, adding a redundant method that implies toString is the intended serialisation path when label() is the documented API
evidence: '@Override public String toString() { return label; }' — returns the same value as label()
Javadoc on the class says 'Callers should prefer label() or toString() over name()' — but if toString returns label, listing both alternatives implies they are different when they are identical
No caller in production code uses toString() on a RelationEdgeType — all callers invoke label() explicitly
issues.suggestion: Remove the toString() override. If toString is intentionally kept as an alias for label(), add a comment explaining why (e.g., for logging frameworks that call toString). Otherwise the redundancy is noise.

issues.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor applies null/empty lemma guards defensively on executeDependencyPattern and executeDependencyPatternWithPos but not on executeSurfacePattern and executeCqlQuery — inconsistent defensive sprawl suggesting copy-paste rather than designed policy
evidence: executeDependencyPattern: 'if (lemma == null || lemma.isEmpty()) { logger.debug(...); return Collections.emptyList(); }'
executeDependencyPatternWithPos: same guard
executeSurfacePattern: 'if (lemma == null || lemma.isEmpty()) { logger.debug(...); return Collections.emptyList(); }' — guard present
findCollocations: 'if (lemma == null || lemma.isEmpty()) { logger.debug(...); return Collections.emptyList(); }' — guard present
executeCqlQuery and executeBcqlQuery: no null/empty guard — inconsistent; the interface contract does not specify which methods tolerate null
executeCqlQuery accepts a cqlPattern parameter but has no null/empty guard for it either
issues.suggestion: Either apply null/empty guards uniformly to all QueryExecutor implementations (and document in the interface which parameters must be non-null), or document at the interface level that callers are responsible for null-checking and remove the inconsistent guards. Prefer the former: add @NonNull annotations to QueryExecutor method parameters and remove the redundant individual guards.

root_causes: substituteHeadword and the redundant toString() are artefacts of AI-assisted generation where thin wrapper methods are added without checking if the delegation adds value
includeExamples was added to ExploreOptions anticipating a feature; the dead field was not cleaned up after the feature was not implemented — common in AI-assisted incremental development
Inconsistent null guards reflect copy-paste from an existing method to a new one without evaluating whether the guard was needed
likely_symptoms: includeExamples_dead_field is the same finding reported in abstraction_fitness and convention_outlier
substitute_headword_passthrough_wrapper is the same finding reported in abstraction_fitness
possible_false_positives: Main.java banner System.out.println is functional CLI output, not AI noise
GrammarConfigLoader.missingConfigException factory method is awkward but not a pure pass-through — it constructs a specific exception type with a message; worth examining but confidence is lower
